title: Dyeing with ivy berries
subtitle: Blue-grey to soft grey-green shades from Hedera helix berries on mordanted wool
excerpt: Common ivy (Hedera helix) berries contain a blue pigment that can dye wool in the blue-grey to soft lavender range on an alum mordant. The dye is experimental rather than fast: ivy berry colour fades with light exposure and is best treated as a seasonal, decorative dye rather than a permanent one. Ripe black berries only should be used; green berries contribute little colour.
sourceNotes: Pellew, C.O., 'Dyes and Dyeing' (1913); traditional British hedgerow dye practice. Ivy berry dye is consistently described as experimental and non-fast in UK natural dye literature. Observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: A mineral or plant compound used to fix natural dye to fibre. Alum (potassium aluminium sulphate) is the most widely used mordant for wool. It forms a molecular link between the dye molecule and the fibre protein, improving both colour depth and washfastness.
The dry weight of the fibre before dyeing, used as the reference unit for calculating mordant and dye-plant quantities. A 15% WOF alum mordant on 100 g of wool means 15 g of alum potash.

The resistance of a dyed colour to fading when exposed to light. Ivy berry dye has low lightfastness: the blue-grey will fade noticeably within weeks of exposure to strong sunlight. Items dyed with ivy berries are best treated as decorative or experimental.

A natural dye that fades relatively quickly with light exposure or washing. Ivy berry dye is classified as fugitive because it lacks the chemical stability of dyes such as madder or weld. Fugitive dyes are worthwhile as seasonal or experimental projects but should not be used where lasting colour is required.

content.text: Common ivy (Hedera helix) berries ripen in autumn and early winter, turning black from November to February in the UK. The ripe berries contain a blue pigment that dyes wool in the blue-grey to soft lavender range on an alum
mordant
. The dye is best described as a
fugitive dye
lightfastness
is low and the colour fades with repeated washing and light exposure. Treat this as an experimental hedgerow dye rather than a production dye for everyday items. Mordant: alum potash at 15%
weight of fibre
(WOF). Use ripe black berries only; green berries give little colour. Keep the dye bath below 75°C throughout: high heat destroys the pigment.

attrs.title: Mordant safety
attrs.body: Work outdoors or with an extractor fan. Dedicated pots only. Gloves, apron, closed-toe shoes. Keep children and pets out of the dye area.

attrs.title: Ivy berry toxicity
attrs.body: Ivy berries are toxic if eaten. Wear gloves when harvesting and handling. Wash hands after contact. Keep children and pets away from harvested berries.

content.text: Method
Step 1: Pre-wet the fibre

Soak the dry wool in warm water for at least 30 minutes until fully saturated. Lift and check for dry spots: any area that resists water will also resist colour. Set the pre-wetted fibre aside in a bowl of warm water while preparing the mordant bath.
Step 2: Mordant bath

Dissolve 15 g of alum potash in a cup of hot water, then add to the dye pot with 4 litres of water and stir. Lower the pre-wetted wool into the pot. Heat to 80°C over 30 minutes, stirring gently every 10 minutes. Hold at 80°C for 45 minutes. Allow the wool to cool in the mordant liquor before lifting.
Step 3: Dye-plant preparation

Wearing gloves, lightly crush the ripe berries in a bowl to release the juice. Add the crushed berries to a pot with 4 litres of cold water. Heat slowly to 70°C and hold at 65 to 70°C for 20 minutes. Do not exceed 75°C: higher temperatures destroy the pigment. Strain through a cloth, pressing the berries gently to extract as much pigment as possible. Discard the solids. Return the strained blue-purple liquor to the dye pot.
Step 4: Dye bath

Lower the mordanted wool into the strained dye liquor. Hold the temperature at 65 to 70°C for 30 minutes, stirring gently every 10 minutes. Do not exceed 75°C at any point during the dye bath. The fibre will appear a deeper blue-grey in the bath than on the dried result; expect the colour to lighten on drying. Remove from the heat and allow the wool to cool in the bath for 20 minutes before lifting.
Step 5: Rinse and dry

Rinse the wool in water at a similar temperature to the dye bath, then in progressively cooler water until the water runs close to clear. Some colour release is normal with this dye. Squeeze gently and hang in a shaded spot to dry. Avoid direct sunlight during and after drying.
Colour variations

Alum mordant with ripe black berries gives a soft blue-grey to muted lavender-grey. The result varies with the ripeness of the berries: very ripe berries (almost shrivelled) give a stronger colour than just-turned berries.

No mordant: on un-mordanted wool, ivy berries give a pale, washed-out grey with very low fastness. The alum mordant makes a meaningful difference to colour depth and durability relative to a no-mordant attempt.

pH shift: add a small amount of white wine vinegar (1 teaspoon per litre of dye bath) to shift the colour slightly toward a cooler grey-blue. Add a pinch of bicarbonate of soda to shift toward a greyer-green. These effects are subtle with ivy berry dye and will not rescue low colour depth.
Troubleshooting

items.problem: Almost no colour transferred to the fibre despite a deep-coloured dye bath
items.cause: The dye bath temperature was too high, destroying the pigment before it could bond to the fibre
items.fix: Keep the entire process below 75°C. Check the thermometer rather than estimating. Use a larger quantity of berries (250 to 300 g) for a more concentrated dye bath to allow for pigment loss.

items.problem: The colour faded to near-nothing after one wash
items.cause: Ivy berry dye has inherently low washfastness; this is a characteristic of the dye, not an error in technique
items.fix: Wash ivy-berry-dyed items in cold water only, with no detergent. If any soap is needed, use a small drop of pH-neutral wool wash in cool water. Accept that this dye will fade over time: treat the piece as decorative rather than a working garment.

items.problem: The colour is very uneven with concentrated dark spots and pale areas
items.cause: The fibre was not fully pre-wetted, or the berries were not strained thoroughly and fibre particles contacted the wool directly
items.fix: Pre-wet the fibre until fully saturated with no dry spots. Strain the dye bath through two layers of cloth before adding the fibre. Stir every 10 minutes during the dye bath.
